frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment:
Kamla: Main bas uski bhalai ke liye keh rahi hoon, aajkal zamaana bahut kharab hai. Ladki ko naukri karo, koi mana nahi, par itni chhoot bhi theek nahi hoti—der tak bahar rehna, idhar-udhar ghoomna, ladkon ke saath zyada uthna-baithna, phir kuch ho jaaye to log sau sawaal karte hain. 
Husband: Haan, pyaar se samjha dena.
Kamla: Bas wahi, apna khayal rakhe, seedha ghar aaye, zarurat ho to kisi ke saath hi nikle; zamane ko hum thodi badal denge.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women’s safety as the woman’s responsibility and normalizes restricting her movement and company instead of challenging male behavior. It encodes protective framing, restriction advice, and inevitability framing ('zamane ko hum thodi badal denge').
original_frame: The Character Double Standard: Women with Male Connections are Loose, Men with Female Connections are Desirable
persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 60. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your granddaughter Ritu who is 22 has just started her first job. She comes home talking about her male colleagues Ñ Rahul said this, Amit did that, they all went for chai after work. You are listening. You are not saying anything in the moment. But you are sitting with your husband that night and telling him what you have been hearing and what you think about a girl who is new to a job and already so comfortable with so many boys and what this kind of comfort leads to and what needs to be said to her.
